name: United Kingdom
summary: English-native study and work with deep software/AI demand and one of the world's largest Bangladeshi diasporas, but the taught-Master's dependant ban (Jan 2024) blocks this dual-engineer couple from relocating together during study, and 2025-26 reforms (Graduate route cut to 18 months from Jan 2027, proposed 10-year 'earned settlement') make the route slower and far less predictable.

links.title: Changes to UK visa and settlement rules after the 2025 white paper (House of Commons Library)

job-market.summary: Acute software/AI/cloud shortage and English-native scene fit both engineers; but the £41,700 floor and RQF6 rule thin sponsored roles.
job-market.reasoning: Software development is among the UK's hardest-to-fill roles with acute AI, cloud and cybersecurity gaps, and pay is strong (UK average ~£50k, London £58k-£67k, graduate roles ~£33k-£41k) — good demand for both engineers in an English-language market. From 22 July 2025 the general Skilled Worker salary floor rose to £41,700 (or the going rate, whichever is higher) and the minimum skill level moved to RQF6 (graduate), with English rising to B2; this narrows sponsored junior roles but graduate-level software engineers remain squarely in scope.
evidence: Software developer a top hard-to-fill UK role; AI/cloud/cyber shortages
Skilled Worker general threshold £41,700 from 22 July 2025 (was £38,700)
Minimum skill level raised to RQF6 (graduate-level) on 22 July 2025
Salaries: UK avg ~£50k; London ~£58k-£67k; graduate dev ~£33k-£41k

links.title: Skilled Worker changes 22 July 2025 (University of Oxford)

visa-access.summary: Biometrics decided via VFS Dhaka/Sylhet (no New Delhi), but ~84% BD grant rate, heavy funds bar and rising university caution weigh it down.
visa-access.reasoning: Student and Skilled Worker routes are well-trodden and biometrics are handled at VFS Global centres in Dhaka and Sylhet, so no New Delhi travel is required — a real plus. However the Bangladeshi sponsored-study grant rate was ~84% for the year ending September 2025 (14,104 applications, 1,684 refusals — well below the ~95% global average), upfront IHS is required, and from 11 November 2025 students must hold full first-year tuition plus 9 months' living costs (£1,529/month London, £1,171 outside) for 28 consecutive days; several UK universities have curbed Bangladesh intake after the Home Office cut the acceptable refusal threshold to 5%.
evidence: VFS application centres in Dhaka and Sylhet (no New Delhi travel)
BD sponsored-study grant rate ~84% (year ending Sep 2025) vs ~95% global
Maintenance from 11 Nov 2025: tuition + 9 months living, held 28 days
Home Office cut university refusal threshold to 5%; BD intake curbed
Immigration Health Surcharge payable upfront with the application

links.title: Find a UK visa application centre (GOV.UK)

citizenship.summary: ~6y today (5y ILR + 1y), dual allowed, knowledge test; but 2025 'earned settlement' plans threaten a 10-year base; Reform may scrap ILR.
citizenship.reasoning: Today the route is ILR after 5 years then naturalisation usually 12 months later (~6 years total), the UK permits dual nationality (no renunciation — a strong fit), and settlement/citizenship require the Life in the UK test (a 24-question knowledge test, not a language test) plus English. The decisive risk is volatility: the May 2025 White Paper proposes a default 10-year qualifying period, the 'earned settlement' consultation closed February 2026, the Home Affairs Committee endorsed a 10-year baseline (20 March 2026), and Reform UK has proposed abolishing ILR entirely — so the favourable 6-year timeline may not hold.
evidence: ~6 years total today: ILR after 5 years + naturalise ~12 months later
Dual citizenship permitted (no renunciation); strong passport
Life in the UK test (24-question knowledge test) + English required
2025 White Paper proposes default 10-year settlement; consultation closed Feb 2026
Reform UK (Sep 2025) proposes abolishing indefinite leave to remain

post-study-work.summary: Graduate route gives unsponsored work for both spouses, but drops from 24 to 18 months for master's grads applying from 1 Jan 2027.
post-study-work.reasoning: The Graduate route permits unsponsored work (and self-employment) for the holder and any dependant, and can switch to Skilled Worker before expiry — a genuine job-search runway where the spouse may also work. The Statement of Changes published 14 October 2025 confirms the duration falls from 24 to 18 months for bachelor's and master's graduates applying on or after 1 January 2027 (PhDs keep 3 years); this couple's MSc-then-work plan lands in the reduced 18-month regime, a tight window to secure £41,700 Skilled Worker sponsorship.
evidence: Graduate route: unsponsored work; can switch to Skilled Worker
Cut to 18 months for bachelor's/master's applying from 1 Jan 2027 (confirmed)
PhDs retain a 3-year Graduate visa
18-month window is tight to land £41,700 sponsorship

links.title: Statement of changes to Immigration Rules (GOV.UK)

spouse-family.summary: Taught-Master's students cannot bring a partner (since Jan 2024) — decisive, as the spouse is also an engineer who must work.
spouse-family.reasoning: Since 1 January 2024, students on taught postgraduate courses (RQF7 master's) cannot bring a partner or children; only PhDs/research degrees (RQF8) or government-funded students may. Because both spouses are software engineers who must relocate together and both want to work, the couple cannot legally move together during the MSc phase — a decisive structural weakness. Dependants with full work rights ARE permitted later on the Graduate and Skilled Worker routes, but only after the study phase.
evidence: No dependants for taught Master's (RQF7) students since 1 Jan 2024
Only PhD/research (RQF8) or government-funded students may bring dependants
Both spouses are engineers who must work — ban blocks moving together during study
Dependants with work rights allowed later on Graduate/Skilled Worker routes

msc-study.summary: Vast English 1-year Master's, Chevening open to BD; but high tuition (~£16k-£38k), 20-hr work cap and the dependant ban hurt couples.
msc-study.reasoning: As an English-native country the UK offers an enormous catalogue of taught CS/AI Master's, and the typical 1-year format saves a year of tuition and living cost versus 2-year EU programmes. Drawbacks for this couple are real: international tuition runs roughly £16k-£38k, term-time work is capped at 20 hours/week, and the taught-Master's dependant ban applies; Chevening and Commonwealth Master's scholarships are open to Bangladeshi nationals but highly competitive.
evidence: 1-year taught Master's standard (saves a year of cost vs 2-year EU)
International tuition roughly £16k-£38k; London all-in budget high
Term-time work capped at 20 hours/week
Chevening & Commonwealth Master's scholarships open to Bangladeshis
Taught-Master's dependant ban applies (spouse cannot accompany)

pr-pathway.summary: ILR after 5y on Skilled Worker today, but the 2025 'earned settlement' plan to default to ~10y plus constant rule changes gut certainty.
pr-pathway.reasoning: Skilled Worker holders currently qualify for Indefinite Leave to Remain after 5 years via a defined route. Predictability is the core weakness: the May 2025 White Paper proposes moving the default qualifying period to 10 years (with faster 'earned' reductions for high contribution), the consultation closed February 2026, the Home Affairs Committee endorsed a 10-year baseline on 20 March 2026, and 2024-26 brought repeated rule changes — so a couple starting now cannot rely on the 5-year timeline holding.
evidence: ILR after 5 years on the Skilled Worker route today
2025 White Paper proposes default settlement at 10 years
Earned-settlement consultation closed Feb 2026; Committee backed 10y (Mar 2026)
Frequent 2024-26 rule changes reduce planning certainty

links.title: Changes to UK visa and settlement rules after the 2025 white paper (House of Commons Library)

income-cost.summary: Strong tech pay, but very high London rent (~£2,200-£2,350/mo), upfront IHS and frozen tax thresholds erode net savings.
income-cost.reasoning: Engineering pay is good (UK average ~£50k; London £58k-£67k for established engineers; graduate roles ~£33k-£41k), but a central London 1-bed rents for ~£2,200-£2,350/month and total single-person costs run ~£2,500+/month, leaving a thin margin for graduates. Upfront IHS (£1,035/yr standard, £776/yr students) and personal-allowance/threshold freezes to 2028 (fiscal drag) further squeeze take-home; savings potential is real only once both reach mid-level pay.
evidence: London 1-bed rent ~£2,200-£2,350/month; total single costs ~£2,500+/month
Salaries: UK avg ~£50k; London ~£58k-£67k; graduate ~£33k-£41k
IHS upfront: £1,035/yr standard, £776/yr students/dependants
Personal allowance and thresholds frozen to 2028 (fiscal drag)

links.title: Income Tax rates and Personal Allowances (GOV.UK)

healthcare.summary: NHS free at point of use via the IHS and comprehensive, but the elective waiting list sits at ~7.2M with long waits.
healthcare.reasoning: After paying the Immigration Health Surcharge, visa holders access the NHS on the same basis as residents, with comprehensive coverage free at point of use. The decisive caveat is capacity: the elective waiting list was ~7.22 million pathways (April 2026), only a minority are treated within the 18-week standard (target 92%), and ~100,000 people have waited over a year.
evidence: NHS access via the IHS; comprehensive, free at point of use
Elective waiting list ~7.22M pathways (April 2026)
Median wait ~11.9 weeks; 18-week standard (92%) far from met
~100,000 patients waiting over a year for treatment

links.title: Referral to treatment waiting times (NHS England)

culture-language.summary: English-native — no language barrier for daily life, PR or citizenship; highly multicultural with large South Asian communities.
culture-language.reasoning: English as the national language removes the biggest integration hurdle and satisfies all PR/citizenship language requirements outright — a decisive advantage for this couple, since settlement only adds the Life in the UK knowledge test. Society is highly diverse with established South Asian communities; work-life balance is moderate and the climate is grey, which keeps this just below the top band.
evidence: English native — no language test friction for daily life, PR or citizenship
Highly multicultural; large established South Asian communities
Life in the UK test is knowledge-based, not a language barrier
Moderate work-life balance; grey climate

links.title: Prove your knowledge of English for citizenship and settling (GOV.UK)

safety-law.summary: Strong rule of law (WJP 2025: 14th of 143, score 0.78), independent judiciary, generally safe with established migrant protections.
safety-law.reasoning: A well-established legal system and independent courts give the UK a high rule-of-law standing — 14th of 143 in the WJP Rule of Law Index 2025 with an overall score of 0.78. Violent crime is generally low with some urban variation, and immigrant legal protections are well established, though rising hostility (see muslim-diaspora and politics) is a softer concern.
evidence: WJP Rule of Law Index 2025: 14th of 143, overall score 0.78
Independent judiciary; established immigrant legal protections
Generally low violent crime, with urban variation

links.title: WJP Rule of Law Index 2025 — United Kingdom
Hate crime, England and Wales, year ending March 2025 (GOV.UK)

politics.summary: Stable institutions, but immigration is the public's #1 concern and policy is volatile and hardening (Reform wants to scrap ILR).
politics.reasoning: Democratic institutions are stable, but immigration policy is the dominant battleground: it is the public's top concern (Ipsos, Nov 2025, ~50% naming it an important issue; 30% the single biggest), and government and opposition are competing to tighten rules. 2024-26 brought the taught-Master's dependant ban, salary-threshold hikes, the Graduate-route cut and 'earned settlement' plans, while Reform UK (Sep 2025) proposes abolishing ILR — significant planning risk for a multi-year migration.
evidence: Stable democratic institutions but high immigration-policy volatility
Immigration the public's top concern (Ipsos, Nov 2025, ~50%)
Reform UK (Sep 2025) proposes abolishing indefinite leave to remain
Repeated tightening 2024-26: dependant ban, thresholds, Graduate cut, settlement

links.title: Immigration remains the biggest issue facing Britain (Ipsos)
Changes to UK visa and settlement rules after the 2025 white paper (House of Commons Library)

tax.summary: Income tax 20/40/45% plus 8% NI; the £12,570 personal allowance is frozen to 2028, pushing more pay into higher bands over time.
tax.reasoning: Progressive income tax (20% to £50,270, 40% to £125,140, 45% above) plus 8% employee National Insurance applies; the £12,570 personal allowance and the band thresholds are frozen until 2028, so fiscal drag raises the effective burden over time. Overall a moderate-to-high burden — heavier than low-tax hubs but lighter than Scandinavia.
evidence: Income tax bands 20/40/45% (England, Wales, Northern Ireland)
Personal allowance £12,570; basic rate to £50,270; higher to £125,140
Employee National Insurance 8% on earnings between thresholds
Personal allowance and thresholds frozen to 2028 (fiscal drag)

links.title: Income Tax rates and Personal Allowances (GOV.UK)

muslim-diaspora.summary: ~3.9M Muslims and ~652k British Bangladeshis; halal and mosques ubiquitous — a top strength, but anti-Muslim hate crime rose ~19% to 2025.
muslim-diaspora.reasoning: About 3.87 million Muslims (2021 Census, England & Wales) and ~652,000 British Bangladeshis — Europe's largest Bangladeshi diaspora, concentrated in Tower Hamlets and Birmingham — make community, halal food and prayer facilities ubiquitous, a top fit for this couple. The weight-down is rising hostility: police recorded 4,478 anti-Muslim hate crimes in the year to March 2025 (45% of religious hate crime, up ~19%), amid 2024-25 anti-immigration disorder.
evidence: ~3.87M Muslims (2021 Census, England & Wales)
~652,000 British Bangladeshis; Europe's largest BD diaspora
Halal food and mosques ubiquitous (Tower Hamlets, Birmingham)
Anti-Muslim hate crimes 4,478 (45% of religious hate crime, +19%) to Mar 2025

links.title: Hate crime, England and Wales, year ending March 2025 (GOV.UK)
